nativeRange.habitat: Pantropical species of seasonally inundated wetlands, rice paddies, marsh margins, slow streams, and ephemeral pools. Despite the species epithet 'mexicana', the plant is in fact globally distributed across the tropics and subtropics of the Americas, Africa, Asia, and Australia. Grows as a small annual emersed marsh herb in the wild and tolerates both submersed and emersed life forms; emersed leaves are bright green and rounded while submersed leaves are needle-thin and may flush green to pink-orange.

decorPreferences: pressurized CO2 injection (25-40 mg/L) for reliable growth
regular dosing of macro and micronutrients including iron
good water circulation to prevent stagnation around fine foliage

summary: A delicate small-leaved stem plant pantropical in distribution despite its 'mexicana' epithet. Grown as a background or midground bush in high-tech planted tanks, where strong light, ample CO2, and full macro/micro fertilization drive fast growth and green-to-orange-pink coloration. Less forgiving than R. rotundifolia.
careNotes: Rotala mexicana is a small-leaved stem plant that has become moderately popular in the aquascaping hobby, especially in the 'Araguaia' form collected from the Rio Araguaia in Brazil. Despite its scientific name, R. mexicana is not exclusively Mexican: Kew's Plants of the World Online lists it as native across the Americas (Mexico, Central America, much of South America), tropical Africa, South and South-East Asia, Japan, Korea, and northern Australia — a true pantropical annual marsh herb. Multiple synonyms exist (notably Rotala pusilla and Rotala pygmaea), and various authors have shuffled the plant between Rotala and Ammannia over time. Compared to R. rotundifolia, R. mexicana has noticeably smaller, narrower leaves and a much more demanding care profile. Sources are unusually consistent on the headline message: Flowgrow rates this as 'difficult'; Buce Plant warns the species 'tends to thrive or die off completely' and that any shortfall in light, CO2, or nutrients produces blackened, stunted, or crippled growth; Aqualand Pets Plus rates care as moderate-to-difficult. Recommended setup is high PAR full-spectrum lighting (6500-7000K, 8-10 h/day), pressurized CO2 at 25-40 mg/L, and a balanced macro + micro dosing regime on a nutrient-rich aquasoil substrate. Temperature range across sources is 18-30 °C with a comfortable 22-28 °C; pH tolerates a wide 4.5-7.5 but most sources cluster on slightly acidic (5.0-7.0) for best growth. Carbonate hardness should stay low (0-7 dKH). The 'Araguaia' variety is the most widely traded form: it has fine, pointy, yellowish-green to orange-pink leaves on red-flushed stems, and like other Rotala species attains its strongest red colour under bright light combined with limited nitrate (around 5 ppm) and abundant iron/micronutrients. Placement is background or, with frequent trimming, a midground hedge in nano and mid-sized scapes. Cuttings handle gently — the small fragile leaves bruise easily and abrupt parameter swings can trigger melt. This species is best avoided by beginners and tanks without CO2 injection; growers without a high-tech setup will find R. rotundifolia much more forgiving for a similar visual effect.
breedingNotes: Propagated exclusively by stem cuttings. Trim healthy tops 5-10 cm long just below a node, strip the lowest pair of leaves, and replant directly into the substrate where new roots and lateral shoots develop within a few days under good conditions. Pinching the apical bud on remaining mother stems triggers branching for denser bushes. The plant can also be grown emersed (where its leaves transform into a bright-green rounded form) for nursery propagation or to recover stressed cultures, then re-acclimated to submersed conditions slowly to avoid melt.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for family (Lythraceae), pantropical native range (South America with Central America, Africa, Australia), height (4-12 inches / ~10-30 cm), high lighting, medium-to-high CO2, fast growth, background-with-midground-option placement, nutrient-rich substrate, macro+micro fertilization, propagation by cuttings, and the critical 'thrive or die' care warning (blackening/stunting if any of light, CO2, nutrients is low). Also confirms the Rotala pusilla synonym and 'Mexican Rotala' trade name.

additional.name: Kew Plants of the World Online (POWO)
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy: Rotala mexicana Schltdl. & Cham. (Linnaea 5: 67, 1830), family Lythraceae, order Myrtales. Lists the very broad pantropical native distribution (Mexico, Central America, most of South America, much of tropical Africa, South and South-East Asia including India/China/Japan/Korea, northern Australia). Annual, seasonally dry tropical biome. 23 synonyms including Ammannia mexicana (basionym), Rotala pusilla, Rotala pygmaea.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database (Rotala mexicana 'Araguaia')
additional.notes: Confirms scientific authority (Cham. & Schltdl.), family Lythraceae, tropical South American + West African + Australian + Japanese range, temperature 18-30 °C (optimal 22-28 °C), pH 4.5-7, kH 0-7 dKH, CO2 25-40 mg/L, high light requirement, fast growth, background/midground/nano placement, propagation by cuttings, and rates care difficulty as 'difficult'. Also documents the low-nitrate red-coloration technique and the blackening/stunting failure mode.

additional.name: Aqualand Pets Plus
additional.notes: Cross-source confirmation of moderate-to-high lighting (6500-7000K, 8-10 h/day), moderate-to-high CO2, moderate-to-fast growth, midground/background placement, pH 6.0-7.5, temperature 22-28 °C (72-82 °F), height 10-30 cm, propagation by stem cuttings (2-4 inch cuttings below nodes), and a moderate-to-difficult care rating. Reinforces the importance of nutrient-rich substrate and regular liquid fertilizer dosing.

propagation: head cuttings (5-10 cm, cut below node)
